summary:
Stalin's Great Purge (also known as the Great Terror) was a campaign of political repression in the Soviet Union from approximately 1936 to 1938 that resulted in the imprisonment, exile, or execution of hundreds of thousands of Communist Party members, military officers, intellectuals, and ordinary citizens. Stalin used the purges to consolidate absolute personal power by eliminating potential rivals and anyone deemed insufficiently loyal. The repression built on earlier waves of Soviet political violence including the collectivization campaign and the deliberate famine known as the [[event:3333|Holodomor]], but reached new intensity during 1937–1938.

The purges were prosecuted through show trials in which prominent Old Bolsheviks — the revolutionary generation who had made the October Revolution — publicly confessed to implausible charges of sabotage, espionage, and Trotskyite conspiracy before being executed. The confessions were extracted through torture, psychological pressure, and threats to family members. Among those executed were virtually all of Lenin's close collaborators still living, including Grigory Zinoviev, Lev Kamenev, and Nikolai Bukharin.

The military purge was particularly damaging: approximately 35,000 officers were shot, imprisoned, or dismissed, including three of the five Soviet marshals and most of the senior command. This decapitation of military leadership is widely regarded as a significant factor in the Soviet army's catastrophic initial performance in the [[event:2657|German invasion]] of 1941. The full scale of the repression became clear only after Stalin's death, when Khrushchev's Secret Speech in 1956 acknowledged the crimes.
